data.title: NBA figures weigh in on tensions in Minnesota after Alex Pretti's shooting
data.description:
The fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by a Customs and Border Patrol (CBP) agent in Minneapolis on Saturday forced the postponement of the Minnesota Timberwolves game that evening, and inspired a pair of NBA icons to weigh in on the latest clash between residents there and federal law enforcement.

“It’s just sad, man,” NBA analyst Charles Barkely said during “Inside the NBA” as the league announced Saturday’s game would be put off due to the growing unrest in Minneapolis. “It’s scary. And I don’t know how … it’s gonna end bad, it already ended badly twice and somebody’s gotta step up and be adults, because man, two people died for no reason.”

Pretti was shot and killed after an encounter with immigration officials who were conduting operations in the city. The federal government has said Pretti, who was carrying a handgun as the encounter began, violently resisted, with top Trump administration officials defending the agents’ actions.

But multiple videos show that Pretti, who was trying to help a woman who had fallen after being pepper-sprayed, was shot in the back after officials took away his firearm.

Pretti’s slaying came just days after the shooting death of Renee Good, who was also killed by a federal officer after officers say she blocked ICE from conducting operations in Minneapolis, with the government arguing in that case she used her vehicle as a weapon.

“We’re being divided by media-for-profit, misinformation. There’s so much out there that is really difficult for all of us to sort of reconcile,” Golden State Warriors head coach Steve Kerr said when asked during a press conference about the current political climate in the country. “And so in times like these, you have to lean on values who you are and who you want to be, either as an individual or as a country.”

data.title: History's hall of shame awaits the Trump regime
data.description:
What a year.  

President Trump finished the first year of his second term imposing sadistic violence on American communities and creating chaos around the world. American families face rising costs for food and health care, but Trump and his family have raked in billions from corruption and self-dealing.

Anyone paying attention to Project 2025’s destructive plans and Trump’s seething desire for revenge against his opponents knew last year was going to be bad. Shamefully, many people helped advance that destruction. Fortunately, many others rose to the occasion to blunt Trump’s efforts.

A full accounting will take years and many books, but within our limited space let’s remember just a few of the Trump collaborators who will fill history’s hall of shame. And let’s celebrate some of the heroes who met the moment with courage and conviction. 

First, from the hall of shame: 

Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.): Johnson says God chose him to lead America like Moses. But he’s acted more like a manservant to Pharaoh. Johnson has led congressional Republicans abandoning their constitutional responsibility to be a check on abuse of power — while shoveling more of our national wealth to the top 1 percent. 

MAGA tech bros: Tech execs who lined up at Trump’s inauguration to pledge their loyalty and money have been more than paid back with billions in contracts, tax cuts and deregulation — all while gutting Americans’ privacy, safety and financial security. The DOGE assault on cancer research and other science will cost Americans dearly. Elon Musk’s destruction of USAID saw the world’s richest man using our government as a weapon against millions of the world’s poorest. Shame is not a strong enough word.  

Stephen Miller, Kristi Noem, Tom Homan, Gregory Bovino, and their machinery of terror: Citizens, children, grandparents — no one is safe from the brazen violence and culture of impunity that Trump and his gang have unleashed in Minneapolis and other communities. It is heartbreaking — and enraging. 

Former and current owners of CBS News: Trump filed a ridiculous lawsuit against CBS over his claim that a 60 Minutes interview with Kamala Harris was edited unfairly. It would have been laughed out of court. But CBS’s corporate owners wanted approval from Trump’s corrupt regime for a merger that would make them even richer. So, they sacrificed the network’s integrity and handed him millions. Then the new MAGA-aligned owner put CBS News in the hands of a Trump-approved editor-in-chief. She yanked a 60 Minutes story about Trump shipping people to a torture camp in El Salvador. (The story eventually did run.) We need a robust free press, not more MAGA mouthpieces. 

Chief Justice John Roberts: The disastrous Supreme Court ruling inventing a broad doctrine of presidential immunity was handed down by Roberts and his majority in 2024, but that ruling and others last year have emboldened Trump’s lawlessness. Bonus call out to Justice Brett Kavanaugh, who will long be remembered for justifying ICE abusing the rights of U.S. citizens via the “Kavanaugh stop.” 

Thank goodness for our heroes: 

The Justice League: The rule of law is more fragile than we hoped but stronger than Trump would like. Thousands of lawyers have urged courts to uphold Americans’ rights and freedoms. Judges appointed by Republicans and Democrats alike have done their jobs and required the Trump regime to follow the law. They remind us how important it is to have principled judges, not political enforcers, on the bench.

Protesters and resisters: I am deeply moved by the solidarity and heroism shown by everyday Americans turning out to protect their neighbors even in the face of official brutality and deadly violence. Our responsibility is not to be silent, and not to comply with tyranny.  

Elected officials: Trump is using the federal government to wage financial and physical warfare against cities and states whose leaders will not submit to his will. There are too many to name — local leaders, mayors, governors, state legislators and members of Congress. On days when hope is hard to muster, I am grateful for every brave and committed leader who is using the power of their office to serve their communities.  

Voters: Trump lies about having the “best numbers ever,” but in November, voters in Virginia, New York, New Jersey, Georgia and across the country resoundingly rejected MAGA candidates in favor of leaders who promised to work for the people, not the president. They have given us a road map to shift our tragic national trajectory in this year’s midterms. 

For all the harm done by Trump’s regime — and they’re just getting started — we have seen that they are not invincible. Let’s make this the year we stop them in their tracks.  

Svante Myrick is president of People For the American Way.

data.title: Almost 810K without power as winter storm pummels US
data.description:
More than 800,000 Americans across the Southeast were experiencing power outages Monday morning due to the weekend winter storm, Fern. 

A total of 808,997 people are without power as of Monday morning, according to PowerOutage.com. 

Tennessee was hit the hardest by the storm, with 252,168 customers reporting outages, followed by 160,783 outages in Mississippi and 126,689 reports of outages in Louisiana. 

Other states reporting outages were West Virginia, Virginia, Kentucky, North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia and Texas.

The Trump administration has agreed to provide federal support to Tennessee, which was among some two dozen states that declared a state of emergency ahead of the storm. Republican Gov. Bill Lee (Tenn.) has also deployed the National Guard to assist in the storm response.

Nashville was hit particularly hard by the icy conditions.

“The combination of freezing rain and ice has impacted the NES service territory overnight, with trees being weighed down by ice causing them to snap and take down power lines. Right now, nearly 200,000 customers are currently without power, and at least 7 broken poles have been reported across our service area thus far,” Nashville Electric Service wrote in a post on X on Sunday, noting that outages could last for a day or longer.

“Our teams are fully staffed and already in the field assessing damage and beginning repairs, and contract crews have been called in to assist. Since yesterday, crews have been operating in continuous rotations, and they will remain on extended 14–16‑hour shifts to ensure we can continue responding to outages quickly,” the company added.

Duke Energy, which operates in seven states including Kentucky and the Carolinas, said most of its outages were reported in North Carolina. The company has said it deployed 18,000 restoration workers across the Carolinas.

“Ice is beginning to collect on trees and lines. One quarter inch can bring limbs down onto power lines, and one half inch can add enough weight to bring lines down themselves. Stay safe, stay prepared, and report outages right away,” the company wrote in a post on X.

Some 12 percent of homes in Mississippi were without power at the peak of the storm’s impact on Sunday.

“The situation is expected to get worse before it gets better,” Gov. Tate Reeves said on Sunday, warning that some places may be without power for days.

“We haven’t seen a storm of this magnitude in terms of ice since 1994,” he added. “This magnitude, this large an area in our state, and quite honestly, this much ice, we haven’t seen anything like this for 32 years.”

data.title: GOP governor after Pretti shooting: 'Enough'
data.description:
GOP Gov. Phil Scott (Vt.) on Sunday railed against the fatal shooting of protester Alex Pretti, who was killed by federal agents in Minnesota while attending a demonstration against the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown. 

Pretti was shot with a phone in his hands; the administration says he approached agents with a 9mm handgun.

“Enough … It’s not acceptable for American citizens to be killed by federal agents for exercising their god-given and constitutional rights to protest their government. At best, these federal immigration operations are a complete failure of coordination of acceptable public safety and law enforcement practices, training, and leadership,” Scott wrote in a statement on X.

“At worst, it’s a deliberate federal intimidation and incitement of American citizens that’s resulting in the murder of Americans. Again, enough is enough,” he added.

Scott, alongside other lawmakers have called on President Trump to speak out against killings in Minnesota and the uptick in violence against individuals who speak out against Immigration and Customs Enforcement. 

“The president should pause these operations, de-escalate the situation, and reset the federal government’s focus on truly criminal illegal immigrants. In the absence of presidential action, congress and the courts must step up to restore constitutionality,” Scott wrote. 

Pretti’s parents said they were “heartbroken” by the death of their son on Saturday and urged the public to rewrite the narrative about the ICU nurse. 

“We are heartbroken but also very angry. Alex was a kindhearted soul who cared deeply for his family and friends and also the American veterans whom he cared for as an ICU nurse at the Minneapolis VA hospital. Alex wanted to make a difference in this world. Unfortunately, he will not be with us to see his impact,” the parents said in a statement posted to the social platform X by Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.).

“I do not throw around the hero term lightly. However, his last thought and act was to protect a woman. The sickening lies told about our son by the administration are reprehensible and disgusting. Alex is clearly not holding a gun when attacked by Trump’s murdering and cowardly ICE thugs,” they added. 

Pretti’s death follows the fatal shooting of Renee Good by a federal agent in Minneapolis on Jan. 7, sparking outrage both locally and nationally.

“The events in Minneapolis are incredibly disturbing. The credibility of ICE and DHS are at stake,” GOP Sen. Bill Cassidy (La.) wrote in a post on X.

“There must be a full joint federal and state investigation. We can trust the American people with the truth,” he added.

data.title: Minneapolis police chief: Federal immigration tactics 'obviously not safe'
data.description:
Minneapolis Police Chief Brian O’Hara expressed concern Sunday over the tactics of federal immigration personnel in his city, a day after a Border Patrol agent fatally shot a U.S. citizen.

“The problem is not that enforcement is happening. It’s clearly the manner in which these things are happening. These tactics are very obviously not safe, and it is generating a lot of outrage and fear in the community,” O’Hara told host Margaret Brennan on CBS News’s “Face the Nation.”

On Saturday, 37-year-old Alex Pretti was shot and killed after Customs and Border Protection (CBP) agents shoved him to the ground. Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said Saturday that Pretti brandished a gun at the agents.

But video of the incident shows the man recording agents with his phone as they pushed a woman to the ground. After Pretti attempted to assist the woman, CBP personnel surrounded him, with one appearing to seize a firearm from his waist before an agent fired multiple rounds.  

O’Hara said at a press conference Saturday that Pretti was a lawful gun owner with a permit to carry. The state also does not restrict individuals from carrying a firearm at a protest.

Earlier this month, an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer fatally shot 37-year-old Renee Good, also a U.S. citizen, in her car on a Minneapolis street. 

The incidents have sparked backlash against the Trump administration’s surge of CBP and ICE personnel to Minneapolis, which began earlier this month amid a federal probe into social services fraud in the state.

Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) have called on President Trump to end the federal presence in the city in the wake of Pretti’s killing. 

Meanwhile, there is bipartisan support on Capitol Hill for an independent investigation into the incident; Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension Superintendent Drew Evans said Saturday that his bureau was shut out of the probe into the shooting by the federal government.

data.title: Glenn Close 'outraged and sickened' by what's happing under 'Trump regime'
data.description:
Glenn Close says she can no longer “sit on the sidelines” after a second fatal shooting by federal agents in Minneapolis, calling out the “inhumanity” and “sickening hypocrisy” of the Trump administration.

“Since what happened, again, in Minneapolis, I just feel compelled to say something,” the “Fatal Attraction” star said in a video posted to her more than 1 million followers on Instagram on Sunday.

“I have been mostly out of the U.S. since this past September,” Close said, reading from prepared remarks, “so I’ve watched, with the rest of the world, our democracy being systematically disemboweled and torn apart, along with the institutions which — in the near past, though never perfect — have stabilized our society and supported the American people.”

“I am outraged and sickened by what is happening under the Trump regime: the cruelty, inhumanity, and arrogance, the voracious corruption, the cowardice, the sickening hypocrisy, the blatant manipulation of facts, and now the cold-blooded murder of American citizens,” the 78-year-old Academy Award-nominated performer said.

“I have felt for a long time that there are thousands and thousands of American citizens with cellars full of guns,” Close said.

“I fear that ICE is giving them the excuse to pull the trigger,” she said, referring to U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. 

Close’s comments followed the Saturday fatal shooting of Alex Pretti. The 37-year-old ICU nurse was shot and killed by a Customs and Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis. 

Pretti’s death came weeks after an ICE officer shot and killed a 37-year-old woman, Renee Good, during the Department of Homeland Security’s immigration crackdown in Minnesota. 

In her comments on social media, Close described humans as a “conflicted species.”

“Since we ventured out of the caves, we have been tribal, territorial and violent,” she said. 

“Democracy is the most idealistic and ambitious form of government because it demands that all individuals participate for the good of everyone,” Close said.

“Never perfect — because we are imperfect — but at its core, the idea that all of us are created equal and have the right to life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness was revolutionary when it was conceived, and it still is,” the actor said. 

“Not the pursuit of enemies, or the pursuit of great riches that means the impoverishment of others, but of happiness.”

“I sense from over here that the great American body politic is stirring, waking up and taking in what’s going on — what the Trump regime is attempting to do to our beloved country and its citizens,” Close said.

“It is waking up and taking note,” she said. “And, mark my words, there will be hell to pay.”

data.title: Trump's credit card interest rate cap is a lose-lose
data.description:
President Trump started 2026 with new fights on many fronts, including one that may even excite the left-wing flank in Washington — a battle with the consumer lending industry. On Truth Social last week, the president declared Americans will no longer be “ripped off” by the credit card industry, endorsing a one-year cap on interest rates at 10 percent.

Legal and constitutional questions aside, although this latest Trumpnomics move may win favor in some corners of the midterm electorate, the idea is not particularly new. In fact, it’s ripped directly from a bill introduced last year by populist duo Sens. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) and Josh Hawley (R-Mo.), which proposes a permanent federal 10 percent annual percentage rate cap on personal credit cards.

Rather than put the hurt on big banks, though, Trump’s move will merely limit many Americans’ access to credit. Others will be cut off entirely. 

Everyone wants lower interest rates, but the question to policymakers must ask is, at what cost? As a pure state intervention into private credit markets, Trump’s move functions as a government price control. History repeatedly shows that price controls don’t protect consumers — instead, they shrink the market, reduce access, and hurt the very people they claim to help.

For tens of millions of Americans, credit cards help cover emergency expenses, smooth out income volatility, and build credit history that unlocks future financing opportunities. The interest rates on those cards reflect the bank’s risk, not moustache-twisting corporate villainy. When lawmakers artificially lower that price, lenders don’t absorb the loss out of goodwill — they respond rationally by lending less.

That’s the inconvenient truth Trump ignores. A 10 percent cap makes it economically unwise to offer credit cards to borrowers with imperfect credit. Financial institutions would exclude them and probably also ratchet down their perks, do away with popular rewards programs, and raise fees for everyone else.

Those who can easily get and use credit will be fine, as always. Everyone else won’t be. 

We’ve seen this movie before. States and countries that impose aggressive interest-rate caps don’t eliminate high-cost borrowing — they just drive it underground or offshore, where fewer consumer protections exist. We have plentiful evidence from recent failed experiments in interest caps in Illinois and in the nation of Chile.

Despite the evidence from both markets, Trump is likely to find quiet support on the left, not just in politics but in academia as well. The most-cited academic contribution on capping on credit card interest rates was produced last year by Vanderbilt University’s Policy Accelerator program.

Their argument rests on the supposed high profit margins of credit card issuers alone, which the researchers argue would be large enough to absorb and even offset the impact of less revenue from interest.

In their view, the economic impact of capping interest rates on credit cards would only be marginal, forcing credit issuers to seek profits elsewhere and passing savings on to consumers. Even in their own analysis, the most enthusiastic supporters of a 10 percent cap recognize this isn’t a free lunch. 

But it is unrealistic to think banks would just trim marketing or rewards. Decades of evidence show that rate caps usually shrink credit access by double digits and harm borrowers’ financial well-being. In practice, this 10 percent credit-card cap would slam the door shut on at least 14 million Americans, particularly those with modest incomes who rely on revolving credit for everyday necessities.

The populist right has joined the left in their ire over credit-based lifestyles, demonstrated by Tucker Carlson’s final show with Charlie Kirk weeks before Kirk’s assassination, which focused heavily on their shared disdain for the credit industry. 

Nevertheless, basic economics remains undefeated. When the government mandates prices below market reality, supply dries up. In housing, that means shortages. In energy, it means blackouts. In credit, it means a perilous squeeze, and consumers feel it first when they can’t get any. 

Enacting new laws or restrictive limits to reduce credit access to all Americans is not a reasonable solution. Policymakers should focus on credit transparency and financial literacy as much more effective measures to tackle the debt problem. Encouraging personalized payback plans, for one, would actually help real people struggling with debt spirals.

Social media influencers and educators like Dave Ramsey and Caleb Hammer, teaching Americans about how to better budget and eliminate debt, are doing wonders for their audiences. Debt can be a real problem for some people, but debt also solves short-term problems, freeing others to make better long-term moves.

Trump is flirting with a feel-good policy that looks compassionate yet functions like a financial freeze. That’s exactly what a credit cap threatens to do — turn a complex consumer marketplace into a government-run monstrosity. 

Yael Ossowski is deputy director at the Consumer Choice Center and author of “The Perilous Credit Squeeze: Why Credit Card Interest Rate Caps Harm Consumers.”

data.title: Columbia picks new president from the University of Wisconsin
data.description:
Columbia University announced on Sunday that Jennifer Mnookin would serve as its next president starting July 1. 

Mnookin currently serves as the chancellor of the University of Wisconsin-Madison, a school with more than 50,000 students and over 25,000 faculty and staff.  

She will be the fourth president in two years for Columbia, which has faced a turbulent time between the pro-Palestinian protests to the Trump administration freezing millions of dollars in its research funding.  

“Jennifer will be an exceptional leader for Columbia’s future,” said David J. Greenwald and Jeh C. Johnson, co-chairs of the Columbia University Board of Trustees.  

“She is a distinguished scholar who now leads a complex institution with clarity and vision. She understands the essential role that higher education and research play in advancing knowledge, serving the public good, and addressing our most pressing challenges. She is also a keen listener who builds consensus and does not shy away from making difficult decisions when necessary. As president, she will lead Columbia forward with strength and a firm commitment to the University’s cherished values,” they added.  

Mnookin, who is 58-years-old, served as dean of the University of California, Los Angeles, School of Law for seven years before she went to the University of Wisconsin-Madison.  

The university has faced years of scrutiny over how it handled the campus protests after Hamas’s Oct. 7, 2023 attack on Israel. Columbia saw arrests and suspensions of numerous students, and later struck a deal with the federal government to restore the Trump-frozen funding, which the administration says was in response to its failure to confront antisemitism. 

During Mnookin’s time at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, The New York Times reported she had the police dismantle the pro-Palestinian encampment after warning protesters. When they came back, she was involved in negotiations that ended the encampment.  

“I am honored and thrilled to join Columbia University at this important moment,” Mnookin said. “Columbia is defined by rigorous scholarship, a deep commitment to open inquiry, world-class patient care, and an inseparable and enduring connection to New York City, the greatest city in the world. I look forward to working closely with faculty, students, and staff, and with both our local and global community of alumni and friends, to advance the University’s critically important mission and to ensure that its teaching and research continue to contribute meaningfully to society.”

data.title: Comer suggests Trump pull ICE out of Minnesota
data.description:
Rep. James Comer (R-Ky.) said Sunday that President Trump should consider pulling Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) personnel from Minnesota, a day after a Border Patrol agent fatally shot an American citizen. 

“If I were President Trump, I would almost think about, ‘Ok if the mayor and the governor are going to put our ICE officials in harm’s way and there’s a chance of losing more innocent lives,’ then maybe go to another city,’” Comer told host Maria Bartiromo on Fox News’s “Sunday Morning Futures,” referring to Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) and Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D). 

After 37-year-old Alex Pretti, a U.S. citizen, was shot and killed by a Customs and Border Protection (CBP) agent, Frey and Walz reiterated their calls for the president to remove ICE and CBP personnel from the state. The Trump administration surged federal immigration enforcement to Minnesota earlier this month amid a probe into social services fraud.

Days after that initial surge, an ICE officer fatally shot 37-year-old Renee Good, also a U.S. citizen, in Minneapolis.

“I just know that the situation’s bad there,” Comer added on Sunday. “On one hand, there are thousands of criminal illegals in Minnesota, because that was a sanctuary city and a sanctuary state. The majority of Minnesotans, in my opinion, want them apprehended and sent back to where they came from.”

“But on the other hand, I think this situation’s only going to get worse and I really think that there are so many cities in the United States that have issues with criminal illegals, that I think [federal immigration enforcement] would be better received in other cities,” he continued.

On Sunday, Trump told The Wall Street Journal that federal personnel will leave Minnesota “at some point.” On Monday morning, the president wrote on Truth Social that his border czar, Tom Homan, will head to the state later in the day. 

“He has not been involved in that area, but knows and likes many of the people there,” Trump noted. “Tom is tough but fair, and will report directly to me.”

data.title: We need profiles in courage from congressional Republicans, not cowering
data.description:
Did you see that?

A video from a Miami nightclub last week showed top-rated, right-wing influencers partying to Kanye West’s Nazi song “Heil Hitler.” The crew included Nick Fuentes, famous for hating Jewish people, and the Tate brothers, famous for brutal approaches to women.

The video went to the top of right-wing social media.

It is being widely condemned as obscene.

But what about the obscene muzzling of Republicans in Congress? Like hostages with their mouths taped shut they are unable to respond to reckless behavior by President Trump.

First, there was fear-filled silence from GOP leaders when Trump threatened military action to take over Greenland. His words led Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney to call for new alliances apart from the U.S. because Trump had ended the “Old-World Order.” 

The Wall Street Journal’s conservative editorial page said Trump’s act was “dangerous,” because China and Russia want to see a “rift that blows up NATO.”

Most of the GOP Congress didn’t see or say anything.

Second, Republicans in Congress are sitting quietly while Trump sends massive numbers of federal agents into an American city. That incursion resulted in an ICE agent shooting and killing an unarmed woman. And in response to protests against the shooting, Trump threatened to send in federal troops.

Again, the loud silence among congressional Republicans is enabling bad behavior. It is another obscenity.

Third, Trump’s Justice Department is going after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell.

Powell announced that the Federal Reserve had been handed subpoenas and a threat of federal indictment for him. He called the possible “criminal charges” against him a crude threat against the agency’s independence.

It is obscene to see so many tongues being bitten bloody among the Republican majority in Congress.

Trump’s frantic, one-man takeover of the government is more in line with a king than a president. But top Republicans in Congress, Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) and Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.), have little to say about his abuse of power. Fear of Trump attacking them on social media or backing a rival in a primary race has them paralyzed. They are fixated on Trump’s dominance of the GOP base.

These “completely self-neutered,” Republicans in Congress, wrote Thomas Friedman, the New York Times columnist, have left Americans wondering if they are being “ruled by a mad king?”

Incredibly, even the most obsequious Republicans are being lashed by Trump. 

Take the sad tale of Rep. Elise Stefanik (R-N.Y.).

She has been humiliated repeatedly for her fealty. She was promised the U.N. ambassadorship, only to be told she had to stay in Congress to bolster the thin  GOP majority. Then she planned to run as a Trump-endorsed candidate for governor. But Trump not only denied her the endorsement but openly embraced her political foil, the socialist New York City mayor, Zohran Mamdani.

Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.) is another Trump supplicant in Congress now being targeted.

A physician, Cassidy provided the critical support to confirm Trump’s pick to be secretary of Health and Human Services, Robert Kennedy Jr. He stood by Trump’s pick despite Kennedy’s long record of spreading vaccine misinformation. Cassidy’s reward for that moral abdication? Trump endorsed Cassidy’s Republican primary challenger last week. And measles outbreaks are surging nationwide.

Sen. Susan Collins (R-Maine) also got nothing for acquiescing to Trump. 

Collins famously defended Trump against congressional efforts to stop his imperial actions by saying she believed Trump had “learned a lesson.” 

Recently, Trump profanely berated Collins after she voted in opposition to his use of the military in Venezuela without congressional approval. He later declared on Truth Social that Collins, along with others expressing concern, such as Sens. Josh Hawley (Mo.), Todd Young (Ind.), and Rand Paul (Ky.), “should never be elected to office again.”

Again, Collins’ only response was mild indignation — but no words or action to confront Trump’s indifference to the constitutional role of Congress.

The few Republicans willing to call out Trump’s behavior are retiring.

Sen. Thom Tillis (N.C.) voiced objection to Trump’s Justice Department going after Powell.

“If there were any remaining doubt whether advisers within the Trump Administration are actively pushing to end the independence of the Federal Reserve, there should now be none… I will oppose the confirmation of any nominee for the Fed — including the upcoming Fed Chair vacancy — until this legal matter is fully resolved.”

That did not prompt Trump to end the questionable probe of Powell.

Similarly, Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska) joined a bipartisan delegation to Greenland after Trump threatened to use military force to seize it.

“Safeguarding our relationships with our NATO allies should be paramount,” Murkowski said. “Greenland should be treated as a partner, not a possession.”

Notably, except for Murkowski, the Republicans who dared to join that delegation are retiring. Tillis and Nebraska Rep. Don Bacon took the trip. They found some courage after quitting Capitol Hill.

The Book of Matthew asks: “For what will it profit a man if he gains the whole world and forfeits his soul?”

There are still ten months left in the 119th Congress.

What we need are Republican profiles in courage, not cowering. 

Where is the end to the obscene fright being displayed by Republicans in the face of Trump’s threats to the constitutional order of American democracy?

Juan Williams is senior political analyst for Fox News Channel and a prize-winning civil rights historian. He is the author of the new book “New Prize for These Eyes: The Rise of America’s Second Civil Rights Movement.”
